chunks.title: Introduction to SHBG and Boron
chunks.summary: The speaker shares their current SHBG level and compares it to a previous level from August. They introduce the topic of Boron in the diet, discussing its sources and the potential effects on SHBG levels.
chunks.transcript: I want to show you my shbg of 69.5 it's still above the reference range and I'll talk about what I'm doing to work on that but in August it was 97.5 so what did I do in between now and August I did a few things I started including more boron in my diet things like dates raisins and plums have Boron there's plenty of boron and avocados you can get a magnesium supplement that has Boron if you want to do it or you can take a boron supplement I prefer not to use supplements if I can avoid them I don't think the Boron is the reason the shpg went down I'll tell you why when I was strictly carnivore I also took a boron supplement and saw the shpg go down for a few weeks but then it bumped back up while I was continuing to take the Boron that's a pattern that I've consistently seen with sex hormone binding globulin and Boron and so much so that many people recommend cycling Boron to decrease shbg now if shbg sex hormone binding globulin goes down and then comes back up and you're still taking Boron this doesn't really indicate to me that it's a boron deficiency I think a lot of strict carnivores may have a boron deficiency there's not a lot of boron in meat though there is boron in bones and there's interesting articles talking about those who eat more boron in their foods have stronger bones and less incidence of osteoarthritis which is not necessarily bone strength but it does have to do with bones because it's the cartilage on the phones Etc so Boron is important in the human diet I think mostly we get it from plant Foods specifically fruit like avocado dates raisins plums that kind of thing and other fruit have Boron as well I think even papaya pineapple all these have a little bit of boron so if you're eating an animal-based diet including fruit in your diet you're not going to be deficient in boron I don't think and I don't think taking Boron is necessary so

chunks.title: Phlebotomy and Iron Levels
chunks.summary: The speaker explains their experience with phlebotomy, including the frequency and volume of blood drawn. They discuss their iron levels and how they are addressing concerns regarding potential iron overload.
chunks.transcript: and then I stopped thinking I'm pretty sure I'm getting enough boron in my papaya pineapple mango Etc so I stopped those things and for the majority of the time since the last blood draw in August I've actually been doing phlebotomy so I've been going to a practitioner who will just take my blood and then we just give it to plants or throw it out so essentially bleeding um out of my vein I'm doing about 200 cc's of blood every three to four weeks or if I can do it more regularly I would probably aim for 50 to 60 CC's per week now I don't have hemochromatosis but if you look at my blood work and I'll go back to that in a moment my ferritin was creeping up my iron saturation was creeping up and my transfer and my transference saturation was creeping up and hat tipped Elliott Overton who made a comment on one of my YouTube videos and said you should think about iron overload I this is something where I've learned in the past few months and it's why I wanted to share this with you guys I never really thought that a ferritin of 250 or 300 was problematic for most humans but I'm having to question that in my own mind now and I think that if I continue doing phlebotomy and my shbg continues to come down as I get rid of excess iron then this may lend support

chunks.title: Dietary Choices: Animal-Based Nutrition
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses their preference for red meat and organ meats over other animal proteins like chicken and fish, citing concerns over nutrition density and pollution. They emphasize the importance of sourcing high-quality animal products.
chunks.transcript: said this I'm not going to stop eating those Foods because I believe those foods are the most nutrient dense and least toxic sources of animal protein we can get most chickens are fed grains most chickens are fed meal that has seed oils which will increase linoleic acid and their fat and I'm not a huge fan of any of that so I don't really want to go to eating chicken fish these are animal-based foods quote unquote but they're just more polluted than grass-fed ground beef especially if you're getting it from a good producer here in Costa Rica I know the guy that raises the cows that I eat there's no glyphosate sprayed on the property there's no pesticides and the meat tastes really good know where your meat is coming from so I think that red meat is still the best source and the organs from cows are probably still the best source now if you can get bison or elk or deer great ruminants are fantastic lamb is fantastic all those are great in my opinion but I think ruminants are the best source of animal foods for humans just because they are eating a diet that is most close to their ancestral true diet diet cows and ruminants are supposed to be grass and if you grass feed and grass finish them that's what they do there aren't many chickens on the planet eating a truly chicken ancestral diet there aren't many pigs on the planet eating a truly Pig ancestral diet if you hunt a wild chicken or a wild turkey sure if you hunt a wild boar yeah great but what we know is that wild chickens wild turkeys wild pigs have much lower amounts of linoleic acid in their fat than do domesticated animals because there's linoleic acid in the feed of these animals so I don't want to eat chicken or pork or turkey necessarily for the majority of my meats and fish has heavy metals perifluoroalkylated substances microplastics I've talked about it on a previous podcast if you choose to make those a part of your diet great I just try to do um what's ideal in my mind and I don't want to make it unreachable for people if you're eating those Foods Chicken fish pork rather than plant-based Burgers kudos to you way better but I'm

chunks.title: Monitoring Iron Labs
chunks.summary: The speaker reviews their recent iron lab results, detailing changes in ferritin and iron saturation levels. They express their goal for optimal iron levels and the implications for hormone health.
chunks.transcript: is 252 so it's still on the high end down from 332 in August again I've done multiple phlebotomies and let's go to the other iron labs again I will go through every single thing on this blood work the total iron binding capacity is up the iron is down and the iron saturation is down from 42 to 31. so all of that moved in the right direction I would like to get my ferritin under 150 perhaps even under a hundred and that will probably be where I change the frequency of Phlebotomy and look to see what happens with the sex hormone binding globulin so my learning from all this is the possibility that even those of us without hemochromatosis genes May benefit from being aware of our iron levels especially when it comes to things like hormones or overall health so I'll keep you guys posted on
